text:
Flee far afield, dissolve, and wholly forget
All you, perched mid the leaves, have never known,
The bone-deep weariness, the fever, the fret
Down here, where men sit listening to each other’s groan;
Where palsy shakes the last few, sad thin gray hairs,
Where youth grows pale, ghost-thin, and fades and dies;
Where even to think is to be brimmed with sorrow
And leaden-eyed, dull despairs,
Where Beauty cannot keep her lustrous eyes,
Nor new Love pine for them past tomorrow’s rise.
source_text:
Fade far away, dissolve, and quite forget
What thou among the leaves hast never known,
The weariness, the fever, and the fret
Here, where men sit and hear each other groan;
Where palsy shakes a few, sad, last gray hairs,
Where youth grows pale, and spectre-thin, and dies;
Where but to think is to be full of sorrow
And leaden-eyed despairs,
Where Beauty cannot keep her lustrous eyes,
Or new Love pine at them beyond to-morrow.

text:
That way, when I look, every result and hard-won glory traces its path back to curl close, forever bound by what it owes.
That way draw hours, months, years—trades, contracts, small storefronts, even the tiniest, most overlooked exchange.
That way pulls every piece of ordinary life: casual speech, chipped kitchenware, local politics, neighbors, old family properties.
That way we travel too, me with my scribbled pages and half-hummed songs, trusting, full of quiet reverence.
Like a father heading to visit his own father, tucking his children’s small warm hands in his as he walks.
source_text:
Thither as I look I see each result and glory retracing itself and
nestling close, always obligated,
Thither hours, months, years--thither trades, compacts,
establishments, even the most minute,
Thither every-day life, speech, utensils, politics, persons, estates;
Thither we also, I with my leaves and songs, trustful, admirant,
As a father to his father going takes his children along with him.

text:
As fair you are, my sweet young lass,
So deep my love for you doth lie;
And I will love you always, dear,
Till every last sea runs dry.
source_text:
As fair art thou, my bonie lass,
So deep in luve am I;
And I will luve thee still, my dear,
Till a’ the seas gang dry.

text:
Other painters lay down thronging crowds, anchor one central figure at their heart,
a gilded nimbus fanning outward only from that singular figure’s crown.
I paint unnumbered faces, though, and not a single one goes without its own soft golden halo,
It pours from my brush, from the living spark of every man and woman, glowing bright, unending.
source_text:
Painters have painted their swarming groups and the centre-figure of all,
From the head of the centre-figure spreading a nimbus of gold-color’d light,
But I paint myriads of heads, but paint no head without its nimbus
of gold-color’d light,
From my hand from the brain of every man and woman it streams,
effulgently flowing forever.

text:
I’m bound for heaven!
I couldn’t tell you when,
Don’t you ask me how, either—
Truly, I’m still too stunned
To even work out how to answer you!
I’m bound for heaven!—
It sounds so hazy, so out of reach,
But I know it will come to pass, sure as anything,
As certain as flocks drift home when night falls
Right into the shepherd’s waiting arm!
source_text:
Going to heaven!
I don't know when,
Pray do not ask me how, --
Indeed, I 'm too astonished
To think of answering you!
Going to heaven! --
How dim it sounds!
And yet it will be done
As sure as flocks go home at night
Unto the shepherd's arm!

text:
If the church would only pass around mugs of ale,
and stoke a crackling warm fire to gladden our souls,
we’d sing and pray straight through every hour of the day,
and never once wish to wander from its walls.
source_text:
But, if at the Church they would give us some ale,
And a pleasant fire our souls to regale,
We’d sing and we’d pray all the livelong day,
Nor ever once wish from the Church to stray.

text:
You sealed my tongue in unfeeling, lifeless clay,
and betrayed me to the weight of mortal days.
Jesus’ death snapped every chain and set me free:
so what business do I have at all with you?
source_text:
Didst close my tongue in senseless clay,
And me to mortal life betray.
The death of Jesus set me free:
Then what have I to do with thee?

text:
They peer into every unguarded nest
where roosting birds are wrapped up warm;
They call at every wild beast’s cave,
to shield each creature from all harm:
If they find any being weeping
who ought to be already sleeping,
they pour soft sleep over their head,
and sit quiet beside their bed.
source_text:
They look in every thoughtless nest
Where birds are covered warm;
They visit caves of every beast,
To keep them all from harm:
If they see any weeping
That should have been sleeping,
They pour sleep on their head,
And sit down by their bed.

text:
When the full morning broke, she had contrived
How she might steal to the old wood on the sly,
How to locate the clay she’d so highly prized,
And croon to it one final lullaby;
That none would mark her absence, unapprised,
While she dared to plumb the dream’s innermost high.
Her resolve locked tight, she brought an aged nurse,
And stepped into that grim dark forest-hearse.
source_text:
When the full morning came, she had devised
How she might secret to the forest hie;
How she might find the clay, so dearly prized,
And sing to it one latest lullaby;
How her short absence might be unsurmised,
While she the inmost of the dream would try.
Resolv'd, she took with her an aged nurse,
And went into that dismal forest-hearse.

text:
ll. 266–7
“Hold Thy turning spheres in perfect, tuned accord”
Speaks of the hymn all roaming heavenly bodies were fabled to pour
As they wheeled their arcs round earth’s still, steady core
Compare the *Merchant of Venice*, fifth act, first scene,
Line sixty, where that same star-song’s trace is seen.
source_text:
ll. 266-7. keep in tune Thy spheres. Refers to the music which the
heavenly bodies were supposed to make as they moved round the earth. Cf.
Merchant of Venice, V. i. 60.

text:
And God, like a doting father, overjoyed to see
His children as warm and carefree as He,
Would start no more feuds with the Devil or the barrel,
But kiss him, and hand him both drink and new clothing.
source_text:
And God, like a father, rejoicing to see
His children as pleasant and happy as He,
Would have no more quarrel with the Devil or the barrel,
But kiss him, and give him both drink and apparel.

text:
Now rose-tinged May bears petal showers,
To grace her cheerful, wide leafy bowers;
And now the bright, glad hours appear,
To roam the glades with my dear Davie.
source_text:
Now rosy May comes in wi’ flowers,
To deck her gay, green-spreading bowers;
And now comes in the happy hours,
To wander wi’ my Davie.

text:
I know now this earth would have been too much to bear,
and even heaven would not have been enough for me;
I wanted only the unshadowed joy,
no cold fear that I had to earn its right to be,
the victory palm without Calvary’s sharp grief,
so, Savior, crucify.

Roused from his drowse, he took her hollow lute,
Fret-rough at first, then drew the gentlest chords,
To sound an old ditty long fallen mute,
Named in Provence *La Belle Dame sans Merci*:
He bent to press the tune close to her ear;
Stirred by the strain, she gave a soft low moan:
He paused—she panted fast—then all at once
Her frightened blue eyes flew wide, shining clear:
He sank to his knees, pale as smooth carved stone.
source_text:
Awakening up, he took her hollow lute,--
Tumultuous,--and, in chords that tenderest be,
He play'd an ancient ditty, long since mute,
In Provence call'd, "La belle dame sans mercy:"
Close to her ear touching the melody;--
Wherewith disturb'd, she utter'd a soft moan:
He ceased--she panted quick--and suddenly
Her blue affrayed eyes wide open shone:
Upon his knees he sank, pale as smooth-sculptured stone.

text:
But peace had worn me so thin I begged in despair,
Till I chanced on that old chap at Cunningham’s fair,
His frayed regimental rags fluttered bright and gaudy,
My heart sang out at the sight of that soldier laddie.
source_text:
But the peace it reduc’d me to beg in despair,
Till I met old boy in a Cunningham fair,
His rags regimental, they flutter’d so gaudy,
My heart it rejoic’d at a sodger laddie.

text:
Shall bold Lapraik, the king o’ hearts,
Tho’ a’ folk were a deck o’ cards,
Roose ye sae true for your right deserts,
Wi’ speech sae friendly;
Yet ye’d hold back your finest parts
An’ ne’er thank him kindly?
source_text:
“Shall bauld Lapraik, the king o’ hearts,
Tho’ mankind were a pack o’ cartes,
Roose you sae weel for your deserts,
In terms sae friendly;
Yet ye’ll neglect to shaw your parts
An’ thank him kindly?”

text:
Leigh Hunt, of their set, wrote his own slim verse
and prose that carried a warm, easy charm.
His encouragement fanned the latent spark
of Keats’s untested genius, but harm
was all his direct imprint left on Keats’s art.

Hunt bore no depth of character or mind;
his verses lean to triteness, cloying sweet,
that loose, conversational cadence he favored
turns grating when applied to themes great and steep.

The small missteps of taste that mar Keats’s early work
doubtless trace root both to this man’s pull
and the unrefined circles of his youth’s small world.
source_text:
One of them, Leigh Hunt, was something of a poet himself and a pleasant
prose-writer. His encouragement did much to stimulate Keats's genius,
but his direct influence on his poetry was wholly bad. Leigh Hunt's was
not a deep nature; his poetry is often trivial and sentimental, and his
easy conversational style is intolerable when applied to a great theme.
To this man's influence, as well as to the surroundings of his youth,
are doubtless due the occasional flaws of taste in Keats's early work.

text:
It fell so far out of my favor
I heard it thud against the dirt,
split to slivers on sharp stones
at the dark base of my mind.
source_text:
It dropped so low in my regard
I heard it hit the ground,
And go to pieces on the stones
At bottom of my mind;

text:
The steep trail unspools at a giddy, disorienting pace
that snags both breath and thought before I can process the sight:
hung in the thin, unbroken blue of open space
an eagle and serpent are coiled tight, locked in fight.
Then the eagle eases off its reckless, tearing flight
just off the high, wind-scoured crag where I stand rooted,
hovers, wheeling slow first to the left, then right,
hangs beating soft, lingering wings over the churning flood,
its ragged, piercing cries splitting the wide air’s quiet solitude.
source_text:
A course precipitous, of dizzy speed,
Suspending thought and breath; a monstrous sight!
For in the air do I behold indeed
An Eagle and a Serpent wreathed in fight:—
And now, relaxing its impetuous flight,
Before the aerial rock on which I stood,
The Eagle, hovering, wheeled to left and right,
And hung with lingering wings over the flood,
And startled with its yells the wide air’s solitude.

text:
He moves through the capitol with effortless, unhurried ease,
drifts among the gathered representatives, one nudging the colleague at his side,
“There he is—our equal, new, fully here at last.”
source_text:
He walks with perfect ease in the capitol,
He walks among the Congress, and one Representative says to another,
Here is our equal appearing and new.

text:
Clouds pressed their broad backs tight to one another
The north wind rose and started shoving hard
The forests galloped fast till they toppled over
Lightning skittered quick as scurrying little mice;
Thunder crumbled apart like some soft, worn stuff—
How good it feels to be safe inside tombs,
Where nature’s violent temper cannot reach,
Nor any cold vengeance ever comes!
source_text:
The clouds their backs together laid,
The north begun to push,
The forests galloped till they fell,
The lightning skipped like mice;
The thunder crumbled like a stuff --
How good to be safe in tombs,
Where nature's temper cannot reach,
Nor vengeance ever comes!

text:
Is that old binding finally gone,
the one that knotted your soft soul to joy?
Did it leave you cracked and hollow-hearted,
stranded in this world’s unyielding cold?
Still, sweet one, worn thin and faint with grief,
even if sorrow poured its weight into your cup,
hold fast to the dream you’ll meet your beloved
and never part again, in heaven’s warm hush.
source_text:
Yet is the tie departed
Which bound thy lovely soul to bliss?
Has it left thee broken-hearted
In a world so cold as this?
Yet, though, fainting fair one,
Sorrow’s self thy cup has given,
Dream thou’lt meet thy dear one,
Never more to part, in Heaven.

text:
Hold nothing back. Speak your thought, don’t be afraid.
Words are only as sacred as the actions that back them:
The priest who renounces the god he’s sworn to serve,
The judge whose rulings make Truth weep at his verdict,
The friend meant to offer steady counsel, as I do now,
but drapes it like a cloak over his own selfish schemes,
The father who carries himself like a ruthless tyrant,
each stains the sacred name they claim to uphold.

I sat watching as the day stretched thin,
gilding spring fields where farmers tucked seed into loam,
the wide unthinking expanse of my land unfurling, lakes winking between dark forests,
the air scrubbed soft and sweet after the last storm’s rough fury,
the arched afternoon sky bleeding slow toward dusk, children’s shouts and women’s calls drifting past,
tides churning their constant restless rhythm, sails dotting the horizon as ships cut through the swell,
summer hanging heavy on the breeze, thick with ripening, every field thrumming with work,
endless separate houses, each humming with its own small rituals—supper simmering, the quiet churn of daily habits,
streets throbbing with their unceasing pulse, cities folding tight around their own bustle—lo, right then, there,
draped over every last thing, tangled up in all of it, wrapping me in the same hush as the rest,
the cloud drifted in, the long black trail unspooling behind it,
and I knew death then: its quiet thought, its sacred, unshakable knowledge.
source_text:
Now while I sat in the day and look’d forth,
In the close of the day with its light and the fields of spring, and
the farmers preparing their crops,
In the large unconscious scenery of my land with its lakes and forests,
In the heavenly aerial beauty, (after the perturb’d winds and the storms,)
Under the arching heavens of the afternoon swift passing, and the
voices of children and women,
The many-moving sea-tides, and I saw the ships how they sail’d,
And the summer approaching with richness, and the fields all busy
with labor,
And the infinite separate houses, how they all went on, each with
its meals and minutia of daily usages,
And the streets how their throbbings throbb’d, and the cities pent--
lo, then and there,
Falling upon them all and among them all, enveloping me with the rest,
Appear’d the cloud, appear’d the long black trail,
And I knew death, its thought, and the sacred knowledge of death.

text:
Yon winding rill that carves the hill,
And skims across the brae, Sir,
Glides past a bower where scores of flowers
Breathe soft scent through the day, Sir;
There Damon reclined, with Sylvia fair,
Their sweet love they held no crime, Sir,
Wild warblers trilled, bright echoes swelled,
As Damon’s pulse kept time, Sir.
source_text:
Yon wandering rill that marks the hill,
And glances o’er the brae, Sir,
Slides by a bower, where mony a flower
Sheds fragrance on the day, Sir;
There Damon lay, with Sylvia gay,
To love they thought no crime, Sir,
The wild birds sang, the echoes rang,
While Damon’s heart beat time, Sir.

text:
The closest dream slips out of reach, unfulfilled.
That distant bliss we chase
Like the mid-June bee
Just ahead of the walking schoolboy
Dares us to join the race;
Drops low to a soft, unguarded clover head --
Dips -- dodges -- teases -- swerves off;
Then drifts toward the gilded upper clouds
Lifts his light, winged little vessel
Paying no mind to the boy
Staring, bewildered, up at the sky that taunts him.
source_text:
The nearest dream recedes, unrealized.
The heaven we chase
Like the June bee
Before the school-boy
Invites the race;
Stoops to an easy clover --
Dips -- evades -- teases -- deploys;
Then to the royal clouds
Lifts his light pinnace
Heedless of the boy
Staring, bewildered, at the mocking sky.

text:
They held so many huddled, jealous talks,
And oft they bit their lips when left alone,
Before they settled on the surest plan
To make the youth atone for his misdeed.
At last, these men of cold, cruel clay
Carved Mercy to the bone with sharpened blade;
For they resolved in some dim, shadowed wood
To slay Lorenzo, and bury him there.
source_text:
And many a jealous conference had they,
And many times they bit their lips alone,
Before they fix'd upon a surest way
To make the youngster for his crime atone;
And at the last, these men of cruel clay
Cut Mercy with a sharp knife to the bone;
For they resolved in some forest dim
To kill Lorenzo, and there bury him.

text:
Bright little joy!
Honey-warm joy, just two days new,
I call out to you, sweet joy:
You tug your lips into a smile,
I hum quiet songs the whole while,
May all soft joy wrap close to you.
source_text:
Pretty joy!
Sweet joy, but two days old.
Sweet joy I call thee:
Thou dost smile,
I sing the while;
Sweet joy befall thee!

text:
Fragments:
The drifter.
Life curved soft at the edges with sleep.
“I grow faint, I waste away for the one I love”
The woman of the southern heat.
West wind, the rouser of what lies dormant.
Rain.
“When mild gales and skies streaked honey-gold”
“and that I walk through the world proudly crowned”
“the rough unruly wind sings its rough song”
“Great Spirit”
“O you immortal, unending deity”
The counterfeit laurel, and the true.
May the limner of quiet light
Beauty’s soft halo.
“The death knell is tolling slow”
“I stood upon a turret that split the heavens open”
source_text:
FRAGMENTS:
A WANDERER.
LIFE ROUNDED WITH SLEEP.
‘I FAINT, I PERISH WITH MY LOVE’.
THE LADY OF THE SOUTH.
ZEPHYRUS THE AWAKENER.
RAIN.
‘WHEN SOFT WINDS AND SUNNY SKIES’.
‘AND THAT I WALK THUS PROUDLY CROWNED’.
‘THE RUDE WIND IS SINGING’.
‘GREAT SPIRIT’.
‘O THOU IMMORTAL DEITY’.
THE FALSE LAUREL AND THE TRUE.
MAY THE LIMNER.
BEAUTY’S HALO.
‘THE DEATH KNELL IS RINGING’.
‘I STOOD UPON A HEAVEN-CLEAVING TURRET’.

text:
My band of brothers presses close at my back
Unarmed, we lock into an unyielding front
We retreat slow, jaws set, brows pulled low enough to cast shade
Even the flush of victory can’t chase doubt from the conquerors’ bones
Our quiet, stubborn will swells as our ranks stitch tighter
We don’t break, we pull back to the grassy hill’s soft cover
Still, our comrades keep getting cut down mid-stride
Their unprotected bodies crumple to the dirt
Their cold, limp forms strewn under every step we take.
source_text:
A band of brothers gathering round me, made,
Although unarmed, a steadfast front, and still
Retreating, with stern looks beneath the shade
Of gathered eyebrows, did the victors fill
With doubt even in success; deliberate will
Inspired our growing troop; not overthrown
It gained the shelter of a grassy hill,
And ever still our comrades were hewn down,
And their defenceless limbs beneath our footsteps strown.

text:
Oh sunflower, frayed and tired of time’s slow drag,
Counting each small step the sun traces overhead,
Chasing that warm, honeyed golden stretch of country
Where every wandering traveller’s road finally ends.

Four odes, all penned in eighteen-nineteen’s span,
the first three drafted when the year first began,
ought to be read as a single, linked set:
one steady strain of thought through all is set
running, and taken as a full combined whole,
they hold the sum of Keats’s reasoned soul.
source_text:
These four odes, which were all written in 1819, the first three in the
early months of that year, ought to be considered together, since the
same strain of thought runs through them all and, taken all together,
they seem to sum up Keats's philosophy.

text:
Will there truly be a morning, after all?
Is there any such thing as actual day?
Would I spot it from the mountain summits?
If I were as tall as those high stone peaks?
source_text:
Will there really be a morning?
Is there such a thing as day?
Could I see it from the mountains
If I were as tall as they?

text:
I tracked the white wave combs as they surged so high, curling over at their sharpest edge,
I caught the wind’s thin piping whine, watched the bruised black clouds mass overhead,
Watched from below as it all swelled and climbed, (O staggering! O wild as my thrumming heart, unruly and unbowed!)
I listened to the unbroken thunder rumble, hot on the heels of every flash of lightning,
I traced the thin, jagged lightning tongues, sharp and swift, darting after one another across the sky amid the roar,
All this, and more like it, I, elated, saw—saw with wonder, still pensive and unyielding,
All the globe’s bared, menacing might rising up on every side of me,
Yet I stood there feeding my soul on it, full, content, haughty in the face of its rage.
source_text:
I mark’d the white combs where they career’d so high, curling over,
I heard the wind piping, I saw the black clouds,
Saw from below what arose and mounted, (O superb! O wild as my
heart, and powerful!)
Heard the continuous thunder as it bellow’d after the lightning,
Noted the slender and jagged threads of lightning as sudden and
fast amid the din they chased each other across the sky;
These, and such as these, I, elate, saw--saw with wonder, yet pensive
and masterful,
All the menacing might of the globe uprisen around me,
Yet there with my soul I fed, I fed content, supercilious.

text:
America watches, warm with quiet satisfaction
across broad Western fields, those slow-crawling marvels
half human ingenuity, half small miracle, tools built to lighten labor’s load
they drift in every direction, thrumming as if alive: the spinning hay rakes,
steam-fueled reapers, horse-drawn work rigs,
threshing engines, grain cleaners that strip straw from kernel cleanly, the quick, nimble work of the patented pitchfork,
and beyond, the newly erected sawmills, Southern cotton gins, the sharp, efficient rice hullers.
source_text:
Well-pleased America thou beholdest,
Over the fields of the West those crawling monsters,
The human-divine inventions, the labor-saving implements;
Beholdest moving in every direction imbued as with life the
revolving hay-rakes,
The steam-power reaping-machines and the horse-power machines
The engines, thrashers of grain and cleaners of grain, well
separating the straw, the nimble work of the patent pitchfork,
Beholdest the newer saw-mill, the southern cotton-gin, and the
rice-cleanser.

text:
Old John, his hair gone soft and snow-white,
lets every worry melt with his warm laugh,
sitting propped against the old oak’s trunk,
surrounded by the rest of the elders.
They chortle as they watch us run and play,
and before long, all of them murmur,
“Ah, these were the very same sweet joys
we knew back when, every girl and boy,
in the bright stretch of our younger years,
ran wild on that loud, resounding green.”
source_text:
Old John, with white hair,
Does laugh away care,
Sitting under the oak,
Among the old folk.
They laugh at our play,
And soon they all say,
‘Such, such were the joys
When we all—girls and boys—
In our youth-time were seen
On the echoing green.’

text:
I won’t breathe its name where the street runs loud,
storefronts would gawk to realize I,
so skittish, so unworldly, so untaught,
would have the nerve to go and die.
source_text:
I will not name it in the street,
For shops would stare, that I,
So shy, so very ignorant,
Should have the face to die.

text:
The taper guttered out as she hurried in;
Its thin wisp of smoke dissolved in pale moon-glow:
She latched the door, chest heaving, all her being kin
To wispy air spirits, and wide-roaming visions slow:
No syllable crossed her lips, lest bitter woe betide!
But deep in her breast, her heart ran quick with speech,
Its unspoken eloquence ached in her soft warm side;
As though a mute nightingale would swell
Her throat for naught, and die, heart-choked, in her dell.
source_text:
Out went the taper as she hurried in;
Its little smoke, in pallid moonshine, died:
She clos'd the door, she panted, all akin
To spirits of the air, and visions wide:
No uttered syllable, or, woe betide!
But to her heart, her heart was voluble,
Paining with eloquence her balmy side;
As though a tongueless nightingale should swell
Her throat in vain, and die, heart-stifled, in her dell.

text:
This is the last of all those soft-spun days,
each bright and beautiful, just as you are,
the loveliest of them all, now burned out,
Rise up, Memory, scribe its well-earned praise!
Do your old, familiar work, etch
the epitaph for glory that has slipped away;
For the earth has shifted its whole familiar face,
and a sharp frown creases the wide brow of the sky.
source_text:
Now the last day of many days,
All beautiful and bright as thou,
The loveliest and the last, is dead,
Rise, Memory, and write its praise!
And do thy wonted work and trace
The epitaph of glory fled;
For now the Earth has changed its face,
A frown is on the Heaven’s brow.

text:
The fairy pointed down at the curving earth.
The spirit’s sharp, knowing gaze
spotted its kin moving through the throng.
From this far vantage, all the swarming crowds
looked just like an anthill’s busy, scurrying brood.
It is staggering, is it not, how even
the sharp passions, blind biases, quiet hungers
that tug at the most unassuming soul, the soft nudge
that thrums along the most delicate nerve,
the half-formed, fleeting thought that flits
through a single human skull, becomes a link
in the vast, unbroken chain of the natural world.
source_text:
The Fairy pointed to the earth.
The Spirit’s intellectual eye
Its kindred beings recognized.
The thronging thousands, to a passing view,
Seemed like an ant-hill’s citizens.
How wonderful! that even
The passions, prejudices, interests,
That sway the meanest being, the weak touch
That moves the finest nerve,
And in one human brain
Causes the faintest thought, becomes a link
In the great chain of Nature.

text:
O brightest of all! Though ancient vows have long passed by,
Too, too far gone for the fond, trusting lyre to glorify,
When haunted forest boughs were held as holy,
Holy the air, the wave, the fire that blazed slowly;
Yet even in these days, so far removed
From the gentle pieties our forebears once loved,
Your lucent fans, fluttering among the faint Olympian throng,
I see, and sing, inspired by the sight my own eyes have drawn.
So let me be your choir, and pour soft lament
Through each slow midnight hour spent.
Your voice, your lute, your pipe, your sweet incense streaming
From swayed censers, rich and teeming;
Your shrine, your grove, your oracle, the warm glow
Of pale-lipped prophets lost in dreaming’s slow flow.
source_text:
O brightest! though too late for antique vows,
Too, too late for the fond believing lyre,
When holy were the haunted forest boughs,
Holy the air, the water, and the fire;
Yet even in these days so far retir'd
From happy pieties, thy lucent fans,
Fluttering among the faint Olympians,
I see, and sing, by my own eyes inspired.
So let me be thy choir, and make a moan
Upon the midnight hours;
Thy voice, thy lute, thy pipe, thy incense sweet
From swinged censer teeming;
Thy shrine, thy grove, thy oracle, thy heat
Of pale-mouth'd prophet dreaming.

text:
They told their sister of the hasty deeds
Lorenzo’d shipped for far and foreign strands,
For their affairs had urgent, pressing needs
That called for someone with reliable hands.
Poor girl, slip on thy stifling widow’s weed
And wrest free now from Hope’s accursed bands:
Thou shalt not set eyes on him today, nor tomorrow,
And the day after shall be steeped in sorrow.
source_text:
They told their sister how, with sudden speed,
Lorenzo had ta'en ship for foreign lands,
Because of some great urgency and need
In their affairs, requiring trusty hands.
Poor Girl! put on thy stifling widow's weed,
And 'scape at once from Hope's accursed bands;
To-day thou wilt not see him, nor to-morrow,
And the next day will be a day of sorrow.

text:
Precious, the boon granted to daydreams,
snatched back even as it’s pressed into our palms:
sweet is the rose that claims home in the heavens,
though it puts down roots in the dust of this world,
where its bright glory unfurls,
even as earth-bound hands rip and rend its leaves,
dying at the very peak of their glow.
source_text:
Dear the boon to Fancy given,
Retracted whilst it’s granted:
Sweet the rose which lives in Heaven,
Although on earth ’tis planted,
Where its honours blow,
While by earth’s slaves the leaves are riven
Which die the while they glow.

text:
The first step of my new learning lit me up so sharp and warm,
just the plain thrum of consciousness, the shape of every thing, the quiet thrill of motion,
the tiniest skittering insect, a stray animal’s soft huff, sight, the low hum of the senses, love,
that first step, I swear, left me awestruck and thrumming with joy so full I ached,
I’ve barely moved past it since, and have no hunger to push any further,
just linger, loitering always, to turn that bright wonder into giddy, ecstatic song.
source_text:
Beginning my studies the first step pleas’d me so much,
The mere fact consciousness, these forms, the power of motion,
The least insect or animal, the senses, eyesight, love,
The first step I say awed me and pleas’d me so much,
I have hardly gone and hardly wish’d to go any farther,
But stop and loiter all the time to sing it in ecstatic songs.

text:
O’, short for of.
O’erword, the chorus; catchphrase.
Onie, any.
Or, ere, prior to.
Orra, spare, extra.
O’t, short for of it.
Ought, same as aught.
Oughtlins, aughtlins, the smallest scrap; at all.
Ourie, trembling, sagging.
Outler, unhoused, roofless.
Owre, above, overly.
Owsen, plow oxen.
Owthor, writer, author.
Oxter’d, supported beneath the arms.
source_text:
O’, of.
O’erword, the refrain; catchword.
Onie, any.
Or, ere, before.
Orra, extra.
O’t, of it.
Ought, aught.
Oughtlins, aughtlins, aught in the least; at all.
Ourie, shivering, drooping.
Outler, unhoused.
Owre, over, too.
Owsen, oxen.
Owthor, author.
Oxter’d, held up under the arms.

text:
Where the young man wasted hollow, gnawed by unslaked desire,
And the pale unwed girl wrapped tight in snow’s white shroud,
Push up from their cold earthen plots, stretch and strain upward,
Toward the same bright height my sunflower longs to reach!
source_text:
Where the Youth pined away with desire,
And the pale virgin shrouded in snow,
Arise from their graves, and aspire
Where my Sunflower wishes to go!

text:
Don’t fool yourself you can heave one soft sigh
And your Maker is not close by.
Don’t believe you can let fall one stray tear
And your Maker is not standing near.
source_text:
Think not thou canst sigh a sigh,
And thy Maker is not by:
Think not thou canst weep a tear,
And thy Maker is not near.
